text.title: Result, Option, and Custom Error Types
text.summary: Idiomatic Rust error handling: Result/Option basics, ? propagation, and typed error enums with thiserror.

rule.statement: Use Result<T, E> for fallible operations; Option<T> for values that may be absent. Never return Result<(), String> in library code — use a typed enum instead.
rule.rationale: Stringly-typed errors discard variant information. Callers cannot match on the kind of failure; they can only print or log it.

antipattern.description: fn process() -> Result<(), String> returning Err("something went wrong".to_string()).
antipattern.reason: Caller cannot distinguish error kinds. Forces string matching which is fragile and slow.

rule.statement: Use the ? operator for error propagation. Add .context() at layer boundaries (one per layer), not on every individual ? call.
rule.rationale: Over-adding .context() on every ? produces noisy error chains. One context per layer boundary ("getting user from DB") is sufficient to locate the failure layer.

rule.statement: Define one error enum per crate using #[derive(thiserror::Error)]. Mark it #[non_exhaustive] on public enums so adding a variant is not a breaking change.
rule.rationale: thiserror derives Display, Error, and From impls with less boilerplate. #[non_exhaustive] signals stability: callers must have a wildcard arm, so new variants can be added.

example.code:
#[derive(thiserror::Error, Debug)] #[non_exhaustive] pub enum AppError { #[error("not found: {0}")] NotFound(String),
#[error("conflict: {0}")] Conflict(String),
#[error("validation: {0}")] Validation(String),
#[error("unauthorized")] Unauthorized,
#[error(transparent)] Db(#[from] sqlx::Error),
#[error(transparent)] Join(#[from] tokio::task::JoinError), }
fn get_user(id: &str, db: &Db) -> Result<User, AppError> { let user = db.find_user(id)?; // sqlx::Error -> AppError via From user.ok_or_else(|| AppError::NotFound(format!("user {id}"))) }
